locked_facts: Area expansion: ΔA = βAΔT where β ≈ 2α
Volume expansion: ΔV = γVΔT where γ ≈ 3α
Water anomalous expansion: maximum density at 4°C
Below 4°C water expands as it cools (anomalous behaviour)
Thermal expansion is caused by increased amplitude of molecular vibrations
Bimetallic strip bends because two metals expand at different rates
truth_statements: When a solid is heated every dimension increases — length, area, and volume all expand
The coefficients of linear, area, and volume expansion are related as β = 2α and γ = 3α
Water has anomalous expansion — it is densest at 4°C and expands both above and below this temperature
This anomalous expansion is crucial for aquatic life: lakes freeze from the top while water at 4°C sinks to the bottom
A hole in a metal plate expands when heated — it does not shrink

aha_moment.what_to_show: A metal plate with a circular hole heated uniformly. The hole gets LARGER, not smaller. Every dimension expands uniformly — the hole expands as if it were filled with the same metal. Students expect holes to shrink — seeing expansion is the breakthrough.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Plate with hole expanding — hole visibly grows, not shrinks — with before/after overlay
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students intuitively expect holes to get smaller when heated — seeing uniform expansion of ALL dimensions including holes corrects this

simulation_states.label: Linear expansion
simulation_states.description: A metal rod heated. Length increases: ΔL = αLΔT. Expansion is small but measurable. Different metals compared (α values).
simulation_states.emphasis: Every linear dimension increases by the same fractional amount αΔT

simulation_states.label: Area and volume expansion
simulation_states.description: A metal plate heated: area expands (β ≈ 2α). A metal cube heated: volume expands (γ ≈ 3α). The relationships 2α and 3α derived visually.
simulation_states.emphasis: Area has two dimensions → 2α. Volume has three → 3α.

simulation_states.label: Hole expansion paradox
simulation_states.description: Metal plate with a hole heated. The hole GROWS larger. Think of it as: the plate expands uniformly as if the hole were filled with the same metal.
simulation_states.emphasis: Holes expand too! Every dimension scales up by the same factor.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student heats different materials and shapes. Bimetallic strip demo: different α causes bending. Water anomalous expansion: density vs T graph peaks at 4°C.
simulation_states.emphasis: Try different materials — see different expansion. Water is special at 4°C.

common_misconceptions.belief: A hole in a heated plate gets smaller
common_misconceptions.correction: The hole expands just like every other dimension. Imagine the hole filled with the same material — it would expand outward. Since the surrounding material must expand by the same amount, the hole grows.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate hole expansion with imaginary filling expanding at the same rate

common_misconceptions.correction: Water has anomalous expansion between 0°C and 4°C — it contracts when heated from 0 to 4°C (density increases). Above 4°C, it expands normally. Maximum density at 4°C.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show density vs temperature graph with peak at 4°C — expansion below 4°C is reversed
